title: AI Ethics & Responsible Use
overview: AI can be unfair and invasive if not built carefully. Bias and privacy are two of the biggest ethical challenges.

blocks.title: AI Bias & Privacy
blocks.body: AI can be unfair and invasive if not built carefully. Bias and privacy are two of the biggest ethical challenges.
bullets: AI Bias — when training data reflects unfairness, AI inherits and amplifies it
Example: a hiring AI trained on historical male-dominated data may reject female applicants unfairly
Privacy — AI systems collect and process massive amounts of personal data
Your data (searches, clicks, location) is used to train and improve AI systems

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Learning from one group
leftPanel.description: If you only learn from one group of people, your opinions will be skewed

centerPanel.label: Programming Concept
centerPanel.textContent: Biased data → Biased model → Unfair predictions

rightPanel.label: Code Example
rightPanel.code: # If training data has 90% male engineers, model may rate males higher

blocks.question: Can AI be unfair even if no one intentionally programmed it to be unfair?
blocks.answer: Yes, because it learns biases inherently present in the training data.

blocks.title: Hallucinations, Deepfakes & Accountability
blocks.body: AI can generate false information confidently and create fake media. Accountability asks who is responsible when AI causes harm.
bullets: AI Hallucinations — AI confidently stating things that are factually wrong
Why hallucinations happen — model predicts plausible-sounding text, not verified facts
Deepfakes — AI-generated fake videos or audio that can spread misinformation
Transparency — people should always know when they are interacting with AI
Accountability — who is responsible when AI makes a harmful decision?
Fairness — AI should perform equally well for all groups of people

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Confident student
leftPanel.description: who makes up answers they do not know

centerPanel.label: Programming Concept
centerPanel.textContent: LLM predicts next word → sounds correct → may be factually wrong

rightPanel.label: Code Example
rightPanel.code: # Always verify AI outputs — especially for medical, legal, financial topics

blocks.question: Should you always verify important facts that an AI gives you?
blocks.answer: Absolutely, because AI can confidently hallucinate incorrect information.

blocks.title: Ethical Steps
bullets: Step 1: Always verify AI outputs before using them — especially for important decisions
Step 2: Check if training data is diverse and representative
Step 3: Be transparent — tell users when AI is involved
Step 4: Assign accountability — define who is responsible for AI decisions
Step 5: Report harmful AI uses — do not misuse AI tools

blocks.title: Practice Exercise
blocks.instructions: Find a real news story about AI causing harm — identify which ethical issue it relates to and write 3 sentences

keyTakeaways.text: AI bias comes from unfair or unrepresentative training data
Privacy is at risk when AI collects and processes personal data
AI hallucinations are confident but factually wrong statements
Deepfakes use AI to generate convincing fake videos and audio
Transparency means people should know when AI is involved
Accountability asks who is responsible when AI causes harm

whatYouLearned: What AI Bias and Hallucinations are
The danger of Deepfakes and Misinformation
Why Transparency and Accountability are important
How to use AI safely and responsibly
